literal: aversion, hatred, repulsion; action-noun from √dviṣ 'to hate, to be hostile' (dviṣ + ghañ → dveṣa), 'the act of hating' or 'that toward which one is hostile'
invariant_definition: *Dveṣa* is aversion or repulsion: the mind's recoil from what is felt to be painful or unwelcome. It is the standing correlate of *rāga* (attraction), and the pair *rāga-dveṣa* names the basic polarity that sets the senses in motion toward and away from their objects. Across the schools the two are treated together as the engine of saṃsāric agency: where the *Gītā* describes the man of settled wisdom moving among sense-objects *rāga-dveṣa-viyukta* (free of attraction and aversion, 2.64), the point is not the suppression of perception but the withdrawal of the appetitive-aversive charge that ordinarily directs it. In Pātañjala Yoga *dveṣa* is one of the five *kleśas* (afflictions), defined as the repulsion that dwells upon pain (*duḥkhānuśayī dveṣaḥ*, YS 2.8), rooted in *avidyā* and feeding the cycle of action and its maturation. In Buddhist analysis its analogue *dosa/dveṣa* is one of the three unwholesome roots and one of the three fires whose extinction is *nibbāna*. In each case *dveṣa* is not a passing dislike but a structural disposition: the negative pole of clinging, to be dissolved rather than merely managed, since while it operates the mind cannot reach the equanimity that the disciplines aim at.

school_framing.shared_core: *Dveṣa* names **the aversive recoil from what is felt as painful, the negative pole paired with *rāga***; the traditions converge in treating the *rāga-dveṣa* polarity as the basic drive of bound agency and its dissolution as a mark of freedom. What varies is the analytic frame — the Gītā's equanimity beyond attraction-and-aversion, Yoga's *kleśa* rooted in *avidyā*, or the Buddhist root-poison — not the phenomenon.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Largely **shared**: *dveṣa* is an agreed-upon affliction rather than a contested category. The chief difference of framing is between the ethical-soteriological treatment (the *rāga-dveṣa* pair as the appetitive charge to be withdrawn) and the technical *kleśa*-analysis of Yoga and the root-poison analysis of Buddhism; all diagnose the same aversive movement of mind.

per_school.definition: *Dveṣa* is the aversive member of the *rāga-dveṣa* pair whose mastery defines the *sthitaprajña*. At 2.64 the disciplined self moves among the objects of the senses with sense-organs freed of attraction and aversion (*rāga-dveṣa-viyuktaiḥ*) and attains *prasāda*, clarity of mind; the verb *na dveṣṭi* (he does not hate) at 2.57 marks the same withdrawal of recoil on encountering the unwelcome. Śaṅkara and the later commentators read the elimination of *dveṣa* not as forced indifference but as the natural condition of one for whom the false superimposition that makes objects repellent has ceased.
primary_loci: Bhagavad-Gītā 3.34 (rāga-dveṣau as the obstacles on the path)

per_school.definition: In Pātañjala Yoga *dveṣa* is one of the five *kleśas*, defined as aversion that follows upon pain (*duḥkhānuśayī dveṣaḥ*, YS 2.8) and rooted, like the rest, in *avidyā* (YS 2.4). With *rāga* it is one of the two affective afflictions; it is attenuated by *kriyā-yoga* and dissolved as discrimination matures, since while it disturbs the *citta* steady absorption cannot arise.
primary_loci: Yoga-Sūtra 2.4 (avidyā as the field of the rest)

per_school.definition: *Dveṣa* (Pali *dosa*) — hatred or ill-will — is one of the three unwholesome roots (*akusala-mūla*: *lobha, dosa, moha*) and one of the three fires whose going-out is *nibbāna*. As ill-will (*byāpāda*) it is the second of the five hindrances to concentration, countered by the cultivation of loving-kindness (*mettā*). It is a defilement to be uprooted rather than a passing mood, here a root-poison rather than one pole of a rāga-dveṣa pair.
primary_loci: the three akusala-mūla (lobha, dosa, moha) in the suttas
the five nīvaraṇa (byāpāda); mettā as antidote
the three fires whose extinction is nibbāna

textual_history.summary: *Dveṣa* (√dviṣ, 'to hate, to be hostile') is genuinely old, but its earliest sense is external: in the Ṛgveda *dveṣas* means 'hostility, enmity, the foe' — what the gods are asked to ward off — not an inner affliction of the mind. The interiorisation into a technical *affect* belongs to the classical period. Pātañjala Yoga makes *dveṣa* one of the five *kleśas*, aversion-following-pain, paired with *rāga* (YS 2.3, 2.8); the *Bhagavad-Gītā* treats the *rāga-dveṣa* pair as the appetitive-aversive charge the disciplined self withdraws (2.64, 3.34). The Buddhist *dosa/dveṣa* — one of the three unwholesome roots and three fires — is the contemporary parallel and is where 'hatred' as a root-defilement is most systematically developed.

stages.sanskrit: prāyus tāriṣṭaṁ nī rapāṁsi mṛkṣataṁ sedhataṁ dveṣo bhavataṁ sacābhuvā
stages.rendering: Lengthen [our] life, wipe away [our] infirmities, ward off hostility (dveṣas), be [our] companions.
stages.note: The earliest stratum of *dveṣa*: not an inner kleśa but *dveṣas*, 'hostility / the enemy' — an external danger the Aśvins are asked to drive off (sedhata, 'ward off'). The word names the foe or the felt hostility between parties, the root √dviṣ in its plain hostile sense; the move to *dveṣa* as a standing affliction of one's own mind is post-Vedic.

stages.rendering: Ignorance, I-am-ness, attraction, aversion, and clinging-to-life are the afflictions. … Aversion is that which dwells upon pain.
stages.note: The decisive interiorisation: *dveṣa* is now one of the five *kleśas*, defined as the standing repulsion that follows upon and broods over pain (*duḥkhānuśayī*), rooted in *avidyā* (YS 2.4) and twinned with *rāga* (*sukhānuśayī rāgaḥ*, 2.7). No longer the external foe but the mind's own aversive disposition.

stages.sanskrit: rāga-dveṣa-viyuktais tu viṣayān indriyaiś caran | ātma-vaśyair vidheyātmā prasādam adhigacchati
stages.rendering: But moving among the objects with the sense-organs freed of attraction and aversion (rāga-dveṣa), self-governed, the disciplined one attains clarity of mind (prasāda).
stages.note: The Gītā treats *dveṣa* almost always as the negative pole of the *rāga-dveṣa* pair, the basic like/dislike polarity that drives bound agency. The ideal is not to feel aversion to the unwelcome but to perceive with the appetitive-aversive charge withdrawn. At 3.34 the two are called *paripanthinau*, highway-robbers lying in wait on the path.

translator_note: 'Aversion' or 'repulsion' is accurate; 'hatred' fits the Buddhist root-poison and the strong cases but overstates the ordinary *rāga-dveṣa* pairing, where *dveṣa* is simply the negative pole of like-and-dislike. The decisive point is that *dveṣa* is treated as the structural twin of *rāga*, not an independent emotion: the Gītā's ideal is not to feel aversion to the unwelcome and attraction to the welcome, but to perceive both with the appetitive-aversive charge withdrawn (*rāga-dveṣa-viyukta*).
